chunk_summary: This section discusses the population burden of wasting in India, with 19.3% of under-5 children wasted and 7.7% severely wasted, and highlights clinically actionable determinants like diet and infections for managing moderate acute malnutrition (MAM).
learning_objectives: Understand the prevalence and classification of wasting in Indian children under 5 years.
Identify key dietary and health-related drivers of moderate acute malnutrition (MAM).
Learn clinical strategies for addressing MAM, including nutrition rehabilitation and illness management.
Recognize the bidirectional relationship between infections and malnutrition.
key_facts: 19.3% of under-5 children in India are wasted, with 7.7% severely wasted (NFHS-5, 2019–2021).
Moderate wasting is estimated at 11.6% by subtracting severe wasting from total wasting.
Key drivers of MAM include inadequate diet quality/quantity, inappropriate feeding practices, and recurrent infections.
Infections like diarrhoea and respiratory illnesses exacerbate malnutrition through reduced appetite and increased catabolism.
Effective outpatient care for moderate wasting can prevent progression to severe wasting and reduce avoidable deaths.
reference_quote: The NFHS-5 (2019–2021) fact sheet reports 19.3% of under-5 children are wasted and 7.7% are severely wasted.
